description: This electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) boundary map shows the grain boundary structure of an Fe–8% Al ferritic low-density steel after high-temperature thermomechanical deformation. Visually, black lines mark high-angle boundaries (HABs, misorientation ≥15°) and red lines mark low-angle boundaries (LABs, 2°–15°). The image reveals elongated original grains oriented along the shear direction (indicated by arrows) that are internally fragmented by a dense network of subgrain boundaries; the 500 μm scale bar indicates the coarse grain scale. The associated study reports that such microstructures arise during continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) at lower temperatures and strain rates, where progressive subgrain rotation and boundary accumulation gradually convert LABs into HABs. The visible serration of prior grain boundaries and subgrain invasion into grain interiors provide direct microstructural evidence for this mechanism, distinguishing it from the discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) regime reported at higher temperatures and strain rates. Overall, the figure supports the conclusion that dynamic recovery and subgrain boundary evolution dominate the microstructural refinement of this alloy during hot torsion, influencing deformation texture and roping behavior.

An electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) boundary map of an Fe–8% Al low-density steel investigated under hot-torsion deformation conditions. Black lines represent high-angle grain boundaries (HABs, ≥15° misorientation) and red lines represent low-angle boundaries (LABs, 2°–15°), as defined in the paper’s EBSD methodology. The map includes a 500 μm scale bar and directional arrows indicating the shear axes. The microstructure exhibits large elongated ferrite grains extensively segmented by red LABs, together with finer grain domains, reflecting dynamic microstructural evolution during high-temperature deformation. Depending on the specific processing window, this morphology corresponds either to progressive subgrain involution characteristic of continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) or to a bimodal mixture of recrystallized and deformed grains associated with discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX). The figure thereby illustrates how hot-working parameters control boundary character distribution and grain fragmentation, underpinning the study’s central thesis that CDRX versus DDRX dominance can be tuned to tailor crystallographic texture and roping resistance in ferritic low-density steels.

This figure shows an electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) boundary map of a thermomechanically processed Fe–8% Al low-density ferritic steel, investigated to elucidate dynamic recrystallization mechanisms under hot torsion conditions. The image visualizes the grain boundary architecture with black lines representing high-angle boundaries (HABs, misorientation ≥15°) and red lines representing low-angle boundaries (LABs, misorientation 2°–15°), based on the paper’s characterization methodology. A 500 μm scale bar is visible at the lower left, and double-headed arrows indicate a reference direction relative to the shear geometry. The microstructure exhibits a polycrystalline ferrite aggregate comprising equiaxed to slightly elongated grains, which is consistent with either continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) characterized by progressive transformation of subgrain boundaries, or discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) exhibiting a mixture of newly recrystallized small grains and retained deformed larger grains. This map supports the central conclusion that the dominant dynamic recrystallization mechanism in this alloy can be identified through the spatial distribution and relative fraction of LABs versus HABs.

This electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) boundary map displays the microstructural evolution of an Fe–8% Al ferritic low-density steel subjected to high-temperature deformation, as characterized by SEM with HKL Channel 5 EBSD. The image distinguishes high-angle grain boundaries (HABs, misorientation ≥15°, black lines) from low-angle boundaries (LABs, 2°–15°, red lines), revealing a complex grain network formed during dynamic recrystallization. A 500 μm scale bar indicates the mesoscopic field of view, while orthogonal arrows mark reference directions within the shear plane. The microstructure exhibits a mixture of equiaxed and slightly elongated ferrite grains (BCC, non-ordered A2 lattice) permeated by abundant subgrain boundaries, consistent with progressive boundary subdivision during thermomechanical processing. Based on the paper's reported experiments, such microstructures are associated with either continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) at 1000°C and lower strain rates, where subgrains progressively transform into grains, or discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) at higher temperatures and strain rates. The dense network of LABs alongside HABs supports the core scientific conclusion that dynamic recovery and misorientation accumulation govern grain refinement and texture development, which is critical for mitigating roping defects during subsequent sheet forming.

The figure presents an EBSD-derived grain boundary misorientation angle distribution curve for a thermomechanically processed Fe–8% Al ferritic low-density steel. The x-axis displays misorientation angle ranging from 0° to 65°, while the y-axis indicates relative frequency. A vertical red line at 15° partitions the distribution into low-angle boundaries (LAB, left side) and high-angle boundaries (HAB, right side). The black curve exhibits a pronounced peak below 5° and decays rapidly with increasing angle, becoming nearly flat beyond 15°. Inset statistics report that LAB constitute 85% of all boundaries, HAB account for 15%, and the average misorientation angle is 9.4°. Within the paper context, this distribution is characteristic of the continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) regime, where progressive subgrain rotation and boundary accumulation dominate. The overwhelming prevalence of LAB and the low average angle contrast markedly with the discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) condition reported in the same study, which yields a Mackenzie-type HAB-rich distribution peaking near 45°. This panel thus provides quantitative evidence for substructure evolution during high-temperature deformation of the alloy.

This figure presents an electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) grain boundary misorientation angle distribution for an Fe–8% Al low-density ferritic steel. The horizontal axis shows misorientation angle from 0° to 65°, while the vertical axis indicates frequency. A red vertical line at 15° demarcates low-angle boundaries (LAB, <15°) from high-angle boundaries (HAB, ≥15°). The plot displays a sharp frequency peak at very low angles (<5°), followed by a rapid decay and a broad, shallow plateau extending across intermediate to high angles (approximately 30°–60°). Inset annotations report LAB and HAB fractions of 47% and 53%, respectively, with an average misorientation angle of 23.7° (denoted as 23,7°). This distribution characterizes the boundary structure evolution during high-temperature deformation, where the coexistence of substantial LAB and HAB populations reflects dynamic recovery and recrystallization processes in the ferritic matrix.

The figure presents a set of {110} pole figures illustrating the texture evolution of a body-centred cubic (bcc) Fe–8% Al ferritic alloy during hot torsion deformation. Subfigure (a) displays the ideal orientations commonly observed in bcc alloys under torsion, denoting key texture components including D1 {112}<111>, D2 {112}<111>, F {110}<001>, E1 {011}<111>, E2 {011}<111>, J1 {011}<211>, and J2 {110}<112>. Subfigures (b) through (e) show experimental {110} pole figures measured at progressively increasing torsion strains of ε = 2, 5, 15, and 25, respectively, with maximum intensity values (in multiples of random distribution) of 2.50, 2.46, 3.43, and 3.17. The intensity initially remains moderate, increases to a peak at ε = 15, and then slightly decreases at ε = 25. These pole figures demonstrate the development and strengthening of specific crystallographic texture components during continuous dynamic recrystallization, with the D2 {112}<111> component becoming predominant at large strains, supporting the conclusion that texture sharpens with strain in the CDRX regime before reaching saturation.

Figure 4 presents EBSD orientation maps showing the microstructures of an Fe–8% Al ferritic low-density steel after hot torsion deformation at 1100 °C to a strain of ε = 2 under two different strain rates. Panel (a) corresponds to a strain rate of 5 s⁻¹, while panel (b) displays two fields of view at 10 s⁻¹. Black lines indicate high-angle grain boundaries (HABs, ≥15° misorientation) and red lines indicate low-angle boundaries (LABs, 2°–15°), with a 500 μm scale bar in each panel. Both subfigures exhibit a bimodal grain structure characteristic of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX): a mixture of small, equiaxed recrystallized grains largely free of internal substructure and remaining large, elongated original grains that retain internal misorientations. The microstructures are dominated by HABs with relatively few LABs, consistent with DDRX rather than continuous dynamic recrystallization. The similar bimodal morphology at both strain rates indicates that DDRX is already active at 5 s⁻¹ and persists at 10 s⁻¹ under these high-temperature conditions, producing nearly random grain orientations that are advantageous for limiting roping defects during subsequent sheet forming.

Figure 5 presents the misorientation distribution functions at 1100 °C for an Fe–8%Al ferritic steel subjected to hot torsion deformation at strain ε = 2. The two subplots compare strain rates of 5 s⁻¹ (a) and 10 s⁻¹ (b). In each panel, the black curve shows the experimental frequency distribution of misorientation angles, while the blue curve displays the theoretical MacKenzie distribution for randomly oriented cubic crystals. A red vertical line at 15° separates low-angle boundaries (LABs) from high-angle boundaries (HABs). At 5 s⁻¹, the LAB fraction is 20%, the HAB fraction is 80%, and the average misorientation angle is 33.1°; at 10 s⁻¹, the corresponding values are 22%, 78%, and 32.9°. Both distributions exhibit broad maxima near 45° and closely resemble the MacKenzie distribution with very few LABs, indicating that discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) has produced a microstructure comprising predominantly randomly oriented grains separated by high-angle boundaries. This stands in marked contrast to the continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) regime observed at lower temperatures and strain rates.

Figure 6 presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) derived orientation maps and the associated {110} pole figures for selected recrystallized grains in an Fe–8% Al ferritic steel after hot torsion at 1100 °C to a strain of ε = 2 under high strain rates. Panels (a) and (b) correspond to strain rates of 5 s⁻¹ and 10 s⁻¹, respectively. In each panel, the left image displays the microstructure where recrystallized grains with internal misorientations below 2° are highlighted in blue, while the right side shows the corresponding {110} pole figure. The maximum intensities are 1.77 m.r.d. at 5 s⁻¹ and 1.66 m.r.d. at 10 s⁻¹. Both pole figures exhibit very weak, nearly random textures approaching a MacKenzie-type distribution, indicating that discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) produces randomly oriented recrystallized grains. This weak texture is advantageous for mitigating surface roping defects during subsequent deep drawing.

This figure presents a strain rate–temperature map illustrating the activation domain of discontinuous dynamic recrystallization (DDRX) in an Fe–8% Al ferritic steel under hot deformation conditions. The vertical axis shows strain rate in s⁻¹ on a logarithmic scale spanning approximately 0.01 to 100 s⁻¹, while the horizontal axis shows temperature from 850 to 1150 °C. Green circles mark conditions where DDRX is not activated, corresponding to the continuous dynamic recrystallization (CDRX) regime, whereas red circles mark conditions where DDRX is activated. A dashed line traces the approximate boundary separating the CDRX and DDRX domains, sloping downward from higher strain rates at lower temperatures to lower strain rates at higher temperatures. The map shows that DDRX is activated at sufficiently high strain rates and temperatures, while CDRX dominates at lower strain rates and temperatures. This competition between mechanisms as a function of deformation conditions is essential for controlling microstructure and texture evolution during hot rolling, as DDRX yields more randomly oriented grains that can reduce roping defects.
